title: Reliability: workflow journal, recovery, idempotency, and retry policy
description:
Swarmstr has turn-level replay/dedupe and fallback behavior, but it does not yet have a durable workflow journal that can resume long-running autonomous work without ambiguity.

This epic adds:
- durable workflow/run journals
- checkpoint persistence and boot-time orphan recovery
- side-effect classification and idempotency keys for external actions
- retry policy by failure class
- crash/replay test coverage for autonomous workflows
acceptance_criteria:
- Multi-step runs can be resumed after crash or restart.
- External side effects are protected by explicit idempotency and retry rules.
- Recovery behavior is tested under duplicate/replay scenarios.
close_reason: All 5 children completed: durable workflow journal (6.1), boot-time orphan detection (6.2), idempotency keys (6.3), retry policy framework (6.4), integration tests (6.5). Multi-step runs can resume from checkpoints, external side-effects are protected by idempotency, retry is policy-driven and side-effect-aware.

title: Recovery: add durable workflow journal and checkpoint persistence for task runs
description:
Create a durable execution journal independent of plain transcript history.

Scope:
- persist step checkpoints, current attempt, last known state, and pending next actions
- decide journal storage model and retention
- link checkpoints to task/run IDs and lifecycle events
acceptance_criteria:
- Workflow journal entries survive daemon restart.
- A task run can resume from a checkpointed state.
- Tests cover checkpoint persistence and reload.
notes: Implemented journal.go (488 lines): JournalEntryType (10 types), JournalEntry, WorkflowCheckpoint, PendingAction, WorkflowJournal with append-only log, checkpointing, FIFO trimming, JournalPersister callback, Snapshot/RestoreFromDoc for crash recovery, FormatJournal. State models: WorkflowJournalDoc, WorkflowCheckpointDoc, PendingActionDoc in models.go. DocsRepository: PutWorkflowJournal, GetWorkflowJournal, ListWorkflowJournals. journal_test.go (792 lines): 35 tests covering all entry types, append, checkpoint, persistence, restore, data isolation, trimming, concurrency (-race clean), JSON round-trips, end-to-end persist→restore→resume. All 406 planner tests pass.
close_reason: Durable workflow journal with checkpoint persistence, restore, and resume. 10 entry types, FIFO-trimmed append-only log, pluggable persister, DocsRepository integration. 35 tests, race-clean.

title: Recovery: implement boot-time orphan detection and resumable run recovery
description:
Resume or mark interrupted work safely after restart.

Scope:
- detect in-progress or orphaned runs on startup
- decide which runs are resumable versus terminally failed
- resume from last checkpoint or emit explicit recovery-required state
acceptance_criteria:
- In-flight autonomous runs are discovered on boot.
- Resumable runs continue without losing task lineage.
- Non-resumable runs are surfaced explicitly rather than silently dropped.
notes: Implemented recovery.go (325 lines): OrphanReason (3 types), RecoveryAction (3 types), OrphanedRun, RecoverySummary, RecoveryConfig with MaxOrphanAge/AutoResume/AutoFail, DetectOrphans scanning in-flight runs via RunFetcher interface, classifyRecoveryAction (stale→fail, no checkpoint→fail, checkpoint→resume), RecoveryExecutor.Execute, PrepareResume restoring WorkflowJournal from persisted doc, MarkFailed with transition audit, FormatRecoverySummary/FormatOrphanedRun. recovery_test.go (791 lines): 30 tests covering all orphan statuses, age checks, auto-resume/fail config, multiple orphans, end-to-end detect→execute→resume, JSON round-trips. Race-clean.
close_reason: Boot-time orphan detection and resumable run recovery. DetectOrphans scans in-flight runs, classifies by checkpoint availability and age, PrepareResume restores journal for continuation. 30 tests, race-clean.

title: Recovery: classify side-effectful actions and add idempotency keys to external execution paths
description:
Prevent duplicate side effects during retries or replay.

Scope:
- classify tools/actions as pure, retryable, or side-effectful
- add idempotency key plumbing where supported
- persist idempotency context alongside checkpoints and task runs
acceptance_criteria:
- Side-effectful actions carry stable idempotency context where possible.
- Duplicate replay does not cause duplicate external effects in covered paths.
- Tests cover repeated delivery and resume scenarios.
notes: Implemented idempotency.go (324 lines): SideEffectClass (pure/retryable/side_effectful), ClassifySideEffect heuristic, IsSafeToReplay, GenerateIdempotencyKey (deterministic SHA-256), IdempotencyRegistry (first-write-wins, restore from persistence), DispatchGuard.ShouldDispatch (pure→allow, completed→skip, failed→retry). idempotency_test.go (506 lines): 35 tests covering classification, key generation, registry CRUD, dispatch guard decisions, concurrent access, end-to-end replay protection and fail-retry, JSON round-trips. Race-clean.
close_reason: Side-effect classification and idempotency keys. 3-class tool classification, deterministic key generation, first-write-wins registry, DispatchGuard for replay protection. 35 tests, race-clean.

title: Budgets: add task/run budget models for tokens, wall-clock, spend, fanout, and tool-call caps
description:
Define the canonical budget contract for autonomous work.

Scope:
- token budget, time budget, spend budget, max child fanout, max tool calls, and optional per-step limits
- inheritance rules from goal -> task -> child task/run
- serialization in task and run state
acceptance_criteria:
- Budget fields are persisted and validated.
- Inheritance and narrowing rules are explicit.
- Tests cover invalid and inherited budget combinations.
close_reason: Added TaskBudget methods: IsZero(), Validate() (non-negative + token consistency), Narrow() (min-of-both inheritance), CheckUsage() → BudgetExceeded, Remaining(). Added TaskUsage.Add() for accumulation. Added BudgetExceeded with Any()/Reasons(). budget_test.go (398 lines): 31 tests covering validation, narrowing, usage checks, remaining, inheritance scenarios, JSON round-trips. Clean build, all tests pass.

title: Budgets: aggregate provider-reported usage and reconcile synthetic estimates where provider data is missing
description:
Replace the current synthetic-only visibility model with the best available runtime accounting.

Scope:
- collect provider-reported token counts consistently
- preserve fallback synthetic estimates only where no provider data exists
- aggregate usage at run/task/goal scope
acceptance_criteria:
- Usage aggregation prefers provider-reported counts where available.
- Tasks and runs expose cumulative usage.
- Tests cover mixed provider/synthetic accounting paths.
close_reason: Created usage.go (253 lines): UsageCollector with per-turn provider/synthetic reconciliation, Cumulative() aggregation preferring provider data, Breakdown() source classification, Summarize() with budget comparison, AggregateUsage() for run→task→goal roll-up, FormatSummary(). TurnUsage with EffectiveInputTokens/EffectiveOutputTokens fallback logic. Thread-safe via sync.Mutex. usage_test.go (368 lines): 23 tests with -race covering provider preference, synthetic fallback, mixed accounting, concurrency, aggregation, summarization, formatting. Clean build.

title: Budgets: enforce budget checks at turn start, tool dispatch, and delegation boundaries
description:
Make budgets operationally real.

Scope:
- refuse work that would immediately violate budget constraints
- stop or block long-running tasks when budgets are exhausted
- prevent delegated child tasks from overspending parent budgets
acceptance_criteria:
- Tasks stop, block, or escalate when budgets are exceeded.
- Delegation cannot exceed inherited budgets.
- Tests cover enforcement on local turns, tools, and ACP delegation.
notes: Implemented budget_enforcement.go (386 lines): BudgetEnforcer with CheckTurnStart (pre-turn gate), CheckToolDispatch (pre-tool gate with projected count), CheckDelegation (pre-delegation with child budget narrowing to remaining), CheckRuntime (wall-clock), CheckAll (combined gate). Warning thresholds (configurable, default 80%), BudgetVerdict (allow/warn/block/skip), BudgetDecision with exceeded/warning dimensions. budget_enforcement_test.go (388 lines): 29 tests covering all boundaries, delegation narrowing, runtime, combined checks, concurrency, JSON round-trips.
close_reason: Implemented budget enforcement at turn start, tool dispatch, delegation, and runtime boundaries. BudgetEnforcer with projected-count checks, delegation narrowing to remaining capacity, warning thresholds, combined gate. 29 tests with -race.

title: Governance: bind approvals, escalation paths, and risk classes to autonomy modes
description:
Replace ad hoc tool-name-only policy with mode-aware and risk-aware behavior.

Scope:
- define risk classes and approval behavior by autonomy mode
- decide when tasks must escalate instead of acting or delegating
- integrate with exec approvals and future verification gating
acceptance_criteria:
- Autonomy mode changes alter effective approval/escalation behavior.
- Risk classes are explicit and tested.
- High-risk actions cannot silently execute in restricted modes.
notes: Implemented governance.go (446 lines): GovernanceEngine with ActionClass (6 types), GovernanceVerdict (4 outcomes), PolicyKey matrix, DefaultGovernancePolicy() covering full 4×4×6 mode/risk/action matrix, hard gates (CanAct, tool allow/deny, delegation), EscalationRequired override, RiskOverride, ClassifyToolRisk heuristic, MayAct/MayDelegate convenience, EvaluateToolCall auto-risk. governance_test.go (600 lines): 40 tests covering all policy matrix cells, hard gates, risk override, custom policies, e2e with authority resolution, acceptance criteria verification.
close_reason: Implemented governance policy engine binding approvals/escalation/risk to autonomy modes. GovernanceEngine with 4×4×6 default policy matrix, hard authority gates, ClassifyToolRisk heuristic, custom policy support. 40 tests with -race.

title: Budgets and controls: enforce token, cost, time, and fanout limits
description:
Swarmstr currently has context limits, turn timeouts, queue caps, and a synthetic usage estimate, but it does not have hard task/run budget enforcement.

This epic adds:
- explicit task/run budget models
- provider-reported usage aggregation where available
- enforcement at turn, tool, and delegation boundaries
- budget exhaustion outcomes and escalation behavior
- operator-facing budget telemetry
acceptance_criteria:
- Tasks and delegated work carry explicit resource budgets.
- Runtime paths enforce those budgets rather than only reporting estimates.
- Budget exhaustion produces visible blocked/escalated/failed states.
close_reason: All 5 children complete: budget models with Narrow/Validate (5.1), provider-preferred usage aggregation (5.2), enforcement at turn/tool/delegation/runtime boundaries (5.3), exhaustion outcomes with mode-aware policy (5.4), operator surfaces with BudgetTracker/Registry/Status (5.5). Full budget stack: models → aggregation → enforcement → outcomes → operator surfaces.

title: Governance: authority model, permissions inheritance, and explicit autonomy modes
description:
Swarmstr already has approvals, DM policies, and signed control auth, but it does not have a first-class authority contract for tasks or explicit autonomy modes that bundle policy decisions into operator-facing states.

This epic adds:
- TaskAuthority and AutonomyMode models
- effective authority resolution across local, spawned, and ACP-delegated work
- policy bundles for act/delegate/escalate behavior
- auditable autonomy mode changes
- enforceable routing/slash/delegation restrictions
acceptance_criteria:
- Effective authority and autonomy mode are machine-readable on every task/run.
- Child or delegated work cannot expand permissions beyond its parent.
- Operators can switch autonomy modes with auditable policy effects.
close_reason: All 5 children complete: TaskAuthority/AutonomyMode schemas (4.1), effective authority resolution with NarrowAuthority (4.2), governance policy engine with 4×4×6 matrix (4.3), auditable autonomy transitions (4.4), enforcement engine for routing/commands/delegation/ACP (4.5). Full governance stack: schemas → resolution → policy → transitions → enforcement.

title: Governance: add TaskAuthority and AutonomyMode schemas to config, tasks, and runs
description:
Define the contract for what a task or agent is allowed to do and how autonomous it is allowed to be.

Scope:
- autonomy mode enum (observe-only, recommend-only, act-with-approval, bounded-autonomous, fully-autonomous, etc.)
- authority structure for tool classes, delegation, approvals, communications, and escalation
- config defaults and task-level overrides
acceptance_criteria:
- TaskAuthority and AutonomyMode are first-class persisted fields.
- Config validation covers supported autonomy modes.
- Tests cover default resolution and invalid values.
close_reason: Extended TaskAuthority with AutonomyMode, RiskClass (typed enum), AllowedTools, DeniedTools, Validate(), Normalize(), EffectiveAutonomyMode(), MayUseTool(), MayDelegateTo(). Added DefaultAuthority() factory for each mode. Added AgentPolicy.DefaultAutonomy/DefaultAuthority config fields with EffectiveDefaultAutonomy/Authority resolvers. RiskClass enum with parse/normalize/valid. governance_test.go (381 lines): 24 tests covering all flows. Fixed NormalizeAutonomyMode to return AutonomyFull on invalid input. Clean build, all tests pass.

title: Governance: implement effective authority resolution across local, spawned, and ACP tasks
description:
Compute the real authority that applies at execution time.

Scope:
- inherit and narrow authority through session spawn and ACP delegation
- merge config defaults, agent defaults, session/task overrides, and parent constraints
- surface the effective authority for debugging and audits
acceptance_criteria:
- Delegated/spawned work cannot widen authority relative to the parent.
- Effective authority is inspectable in runtime state.
- Tests cover inheritance, narrowing, and conflicting inputs.
notes: Implemented authority.go (457 lines): NarrowAuthority with per-field narrowing rules (autonomy ranking, risk severity, boolean AND/OR, tool/agent set intersection/union, depth min), ResolveAuthority for ordered layer folding with audit trace, BuildAuthorityChain convenience builder, FormatTrace for debug output. authority_test.go (716 lines): 42 tests covering all narrowing dimensions, end-to-end inheritance, cannot-widen invariant, delegation layers, legacy normalization, JSON round-trips. All tests pass with -race.
close_reason: Implemented effective authority resolution with NarrowAuthority (per-field narrowing: autonomy ranking, risk severity, boolean gates, set operations, depth min), ResolveAuthority (ordered layer folding with audit trace), BuildAuthorityChain (config→goal→parent→task), and FormatTrace. 42 tests with -race.

title: Verification: add VerificationSpec and enforce acceptance criteria on task completion
description:
Make verification a first-class contract instead of a prompt suggestion.

Scope:
- define VerificationSpec on tasks/runs
- require expected outputs and acceptance criteria for verifiable tasks
- block terminal completion when required verification has not run or has failed
acceptance_criteria:
- Task completion can be gated on explicit VerificationSpec rules.
- Missing acceptance criteria is rejected for tasks that require verification.
- Tests cover successful, failed, and skipped verification cases.
close_reason: Implemented VerificationSpec, VerificationCheck, VerificationStatus, VerificationPolicy, VerificationCheckType models (~215 lines in models.go). Added Verification field to TaskSpec and TaskRun. Created verifier.go (248 lines): Verifier.Evaluate, MayComplete, RecordCheckResult, BuildFromAcceptanceCriteria, ValidateCompletionGate. Created verifier_test.go (578 lines): 38 tests covering all flows. Clean build, all tests pass.

title: Verification: implement generic verifier runtime for schema, evidence, dry-run, and tool-output checks
description:
Add reusable verifier executors rather than bespoke per-domain review logic.

Scope:
- schema validation of outputs
- evidence/reference checks where outputs must cite retrieved state
- dry-run verification for side-effectful actions when supported
- post-tool verification hooks for sensitive tools
acceptance_criteria:
- The verifier runtime supports multiple verification methods under one contract.
- Verification failures return structured reasons.
- Tests cover at least schema-check and evidence-check verifier types.
close_reason: Generic verifier runtime with pluggable CheckExecutor interface. Built-in executors: SchemaCheckExecutor (required field validation), EvidenceCheckExecutor (artifact presence), ToolOutputCheckExecutor (tool success/required tools), DryRunCheckExecutor (dry-run, output_contains, output_not_empty). VerifierRuntime dispatches by check type, handles errors, respects terminal states and advisory policy. 35 tests all passing.

title: Verification: gate risky tool actions and final outputs on verification policy
description:
Connect verification policy to actual runtime behavior.

Scope:
- define which action classes require verification before execution or before final completion
- integrate with approvals and autonomy modes
- ensure failed verification can escalate or return to planning instead of silently continuing
acceptance_criteria:
- Risky actions cannot bypass verification policy.
- Verification failure produces a blocked/escalated/replan state, not silent success.
- Tests cover gating behavior under multiple autonomy modes.
close_reason: Verification gate for risky tool actions and task completion. VerificationGate with ToolRiskClassifier (none/low/medium/high/critical), per-autonomy-mode gating (full→allow/replan, plan_approval→block, step_approval/supervised→escalate). MayExecuteTool pre-action gate, MayComplete output gate. Never silently succeeds on verification failure. 30 tests all passing.

title: Planning: implement initial planner service for goal -> plan generation
description:
Build the first-pass planner that generates a structured plan from a goal or task intake.

Scope:
- create a planner runtime or service seam
- define prompts/contracts for structured planner output
- normalize goals into plan steps with dependencies and completion criteria
- handle underspecified goals by marking missing information instead of hallucinating implementation detail
acceptance_criteria:
- Given a goal, the planner returns a valid structured plan.
- Missing information is represented explicitly as blocked/needs-input state.
- Tests cover valid structured output and malformed-plan rejection.
notes: Implemented initial planner service in internal/planner/planner.go. The planner takes a GoalSpec, builds a structured prompt, calls an LLM Provider, and parses the JSON output into a validated PlanSpec. Features: markdown code-fence stripping, plan ID defaulting, cycle detection, fallback plan generation for malformed output (single blocked review step), model metadata tracking, and underspecified goal handling via PlanStepStatusBlocked. Also added PlanStepStatusBlocked to the plan step status enum in models.go. 14 tests cover: valid output, markdown-fenced output, malformed fallback, provider error, invalid goal, nil provider, cyclic rejection, underspecified goals, empty plan ID, prompt construction, parse validation, fallback structure, and truncation. Validation: go test ./internal/planner/... && go test ./internal/store/state/... -run TestPlanStepStatus && go build ./...
close_reason: Implemented

title: Planning: compile plan steps into executable tasks and dependency scheduling
description:
Convert plan steps into task instances that the runtime can execute and monitor.

Scope:
- materialize plan steps into TaskSpec entries
- preserve parent/child and dependency graph semantics
- support sequential and parallel execution of ready steps
- avoid rerunning completed steps unless invalidated by replan logic
acceptance_criteria:
- Ready plan steps become tasks automatically or through explicit operator action.
- Dependency scheduling respects blocked/completed/failed step state.
- Tests cover parallel-ready and dependency-blocked plans.
notes: Implemented plan step → task compiler and bidirectional state synchronization in internal/planner/compiler.go. Compile() materializes ready plan steps into TaskSpec entries with: deterministic task IDs (task:<planID>:<stepID>), dependency graph mapping (step deps → task deps), goal-level authority/budget inheritance, step agent assignment, expected output propagation, and idempotent re-compilation. SyncStepStates() maps task lifecycle events back to plan step statuses, with automatic plan completion/failure inference when all steps reach terminal state. Also includes taskStatusToPlanStepStatus mapping (in_progress, completed, failed, cancelled→skipped, blocked). 15 tests cover: linear first-step-only, parallel both-branches, incremental after completion, blocked step blocking, idempotency, cyclic rejection, authority inheritance, agent inheritance, sync from task completion, no-change when synced, plan-completes-when-all-done, plan-fails-when-step-fails, cancelled→skipped, blocked→blocked, and TaskIDForStep. Validation: go test ./internal/planner/... && go build ./...
close_reason: Implemented

title: Planning: implement blocked/failure-driven replanning and plan revision history
description:
Add the ability to revise plans when execution diverges from the original assumptions.

Scope:
- trigger replanning from blocked, failed, or invalidated steps
- preserve prior plan versions for auditability
- decide when replanning is automatic versus operator-approved
- annotate revisions with reasons, evidence, and changed assumptions
acceptance_criteria:
- A failed or blocked plan can produce a new plan revision without losing history.
- Operators can inspect what changed between plan versions.
- Tests cover replan triggers and version lineage.
close_reason: Implemented replan.go (290 lines) with NeedsReplan trigger detection, Replan with provider-driven revision, DiffPlans, carryForwardCompleted, buildReplanContext. Created replan_test.go (480 lines) with 19 tests. All 48 planner package tests pass, clean build.

title: Verification layer: QA, evidence checks, and reviewer loops
description:
Swarmstr has prompt-level reminders to verify and a narrow NIP-34 auto-review flow, but it does not have a general verification layer that decides whether outputs are acceptable before they are treated as complete.

This epic adds:
- VerificationSpec and completion-criteria enforcement
- generic verifier step types
- reviewer-worker patterns for higher-risk work
- risk-based gating before external action
- verification results in task/run state and telemetry
acceptance_criteria:
- High-risk or policy-bound tasks can require verification before completion.
- Verification outcomes are durable and inspectable.
- Reviewer loops work across both local and delegated task execution.
close_reason: Verification epic complete. 5 beads: VerificationSpec+acceptance criteria (3.1), generic verifier runtime with 4 built-in executors (3.2), reviewer-worker pattern (3.3), verification gate for risky actions across autonomy modes (3.4), verification telemetry and summaries (3.5). All tests passing.

title: Task model: add control and gateway methods for task CRUD, query, cancel, and resume
description:
Expose the new task model through operator-facing and agent-facing APIs.

Scope:
- add methods to create tasks/goals, list/query by status, inspect dependencies, cancel tasks, and resume blocked work
- decide which methods are operator-only versus agent-available
- align HTTP control, Nostr control RPC, and any WS projections

Likely touchpoints:
- internal/gateway/methods/schema.go
- cmd/metiqd/main.go
- internal/policy/control.go
acceptance_criteria:
- Operators can create, inspect, cancel, and resume tasks through the supported control surfaces.
- Method authorization is explicit and tested.
- Response shapes include enough information to drive planner/verifier and observability work.
notes: Added task control/gateway methods tasks.create, tasks.get, tasks.list, tasks.cancel, and tasks.resume. Extended internal/gateway/methods/schema.go with strict request normalization, supported-method registration, and alias compatibility for task IDs and filters. Implemented durable task CRUD/query/cancel/resume handling in cmd/metiqd/main.go with legal lifecycle transitions, automatic task ID generation, and resume semantics that either re-queue the current run or create a new run attempt. Added request/schema coverage in internal/gateway/methods/schema_test.go and end-to-end control RPC lifecycle coverage in cmd/metiqd/main_test.go. Validation: go test ./internal/gateway/methods ./cmd/metiqd -run 'Test(Tasks|HandleControlRPCRequest_TaskLifecycleMethods)' && go test ./cmd/metiqd -run '^$'
close_reason: Implemented

title: Task model: link tasks to sessions, turns, delegations, and final results
description:
Ensure the new task plane is connected to the current turn/session runtime.

Scope:
- persist task_id / run_id / parent_task_id on session and turn state where relevant
- link ACP delegation and spawned sessions back to parent tasks
- persist final result references and verification placeholders
- avoid requiring transcript reconstruction to understand what happened

Likely touchpoints:
- internal/store/state/session_store.go
- cmd/metiqd/main.go
- ACP history / session spawn / run execution paths
acceptance_criteria:
- A completed task can be traced to the sessions, turns, child tasks, and result artifacts that produced it.
- Delegated tasks preserve parent linkage.
- Integration tests cover local and delegated linkage paths.
notes: Implemented task/session/run/result linkage across local spawn and ACP delegation paths. Added session-store task linkage fields and helpers; propagated task/run/result metadata through ACP worker metadata cloning; created inherited ACP child-task synthesis/binding helpers; persisted canonical worker TaskSpec/TaskRun docs plus session result linkage; attached task/run/result data to ACP turn telemetry; and seeded spawned child sessions with parent task/run linkage. Added targeted coverage in internal/store/state/session_store_test.go, cmd/metiqd/acp_inheritance_test.go, and cmd/metiqd/main_test.go. Validation: go test ./internal/store/state -run 'TestSessionStore_(RecordTurn|LinkTaskAndRecordTaskResult)|TestTurnTelemetry_JSONShape' && go test ./cmd/metiqd -run 'Test(BuildInheritedACPTaskPayloadUsesRuntimeHintsAndContext|ACPWorkerTaskDocs_PersistTaskRunAndResultLinkage|HandleACPMessageAppliesInheritedRuntimeHints|ApplySessionsSpawn_InheritsParentTaskLinkage|ExecuteAgentRun_EmitsAndPersistsTurnTelemetry)' && go test ./internal/acp ./internal/store/state ./cmd/metiqd -run '^$'
close_reason: Implemented

title: Planning engine: plan schemas, decomposition, and replanning
description:
Swarmstr can execute tools and delegate work, but it does not yet have a general planner that turns a goal into a durable dependency graph with revisable steps.

This epic adds:
- PlanSpec / PlanStep / dependency contracts
- planner service for initial decomposition
- plan-to-task compilation
- replanning on blocked/failure outcomes
- plan preview and approval surfaces for operators
acceptance_criteria:
- A goal can be decomposed into a durable, inspectable plan rather than transient plain text.
- Plan steps compile into executable tasks with explicit dependencies.
- The planner can revise a plan after failures or new information.
close_reason: All 5 subtasks complete: plan models (2.1), planner service (2.2), compiler (2.3), replanning (2.4), approval controller (2.5). Full planning engine delivered across internal/planner/ and internal/store/state/.

title: Planning: define PlanSpec / PlanStep / dependency persistence models
description:
Add the canonical plan model that sits above tasks.

Scope:
- define plan versioning, step status, dependencies, assumptions, risks, and rollback strategy
- link plan steps to downstream task IDs when materialized
- choose storage and addressing strategy for plans and revisions
acceptance_criteria:
- PlanSpec and PlanStep JSON contracts exist and round-trip cleanly.
- Step dependencies and revision metadata are modeled explicitly.
- Tests cover validation and revision semantics.
notes: Implemented PlanSpec, PlanStep, PlanStatus, PlanStepStatus persistence models with full validation, normalization, cycle detection, ready-step computation, and revision support. Added PutPlan/GetPlan/ListPlans persistence in docs_repo with goal filtering and cycle rejection. Tests: 12 new tests covering parsing, normalization, validation, cycle detection, ready-steps, terminal state, JSON round-trip, put/get, cycle rejection, goal filtering, and revision updates. Validation: go test ./internal/store/state/... -run 'TestPlan|TestDocsRepositoryPlan|TestDocsRepositoryListPlans'
close_reason: Implemented

title: Autonomy foundation: canonical goal/task/run model and Nostr task envelopes
description:
Swarmstr currently accepts work as turns, session messages, and ACP instructions, but it does not have a canonical task object or a durable run model.

This epic establishes the core machine-readable contract for autonomous work:
- GoalSpec / TaskSpec / TaskRun state
- task lifecycle state transitions
- activation of kind 38383 as a real task envelope instead of a reserved constant
- linkage between tasks, sessions, turns, and delegated work
- gateway/control methods for task creation, inspection, mutation, and cancellation

This is the primary blocker for planner, verifier, budget, governance, observability, and learning work.
acceptance_criteria:
- Swarmstr has a canonical task/run schema persisted independently from raw transcript text.
- Nostr-native task envelopes and local control methods use the same contract.
- Operators and downstream services can identify the current state, owner, parent, and expected outputs of a task without reconstructing it from prompts.
close_reason: All subtasks implemented

title: Task model: define GoalSpec / TaskSpec / TaskRun persistence models
description:
Add the canonical autonomy state models to the store layer.

Scope:
- define GoalSpec, TaskSpec, TaskRun, TaskBudget, TaskAuthority, TaskResultRef, and supporting enums
- choose storage locations and addressing strategy for task and run documents
- define status enums for goal/task/run state machines
- model parent/child task relationships and links to session/run history

Likely touchpoints:
- internal/store/state/models.go
- new task persistence helpers under internal/store/state/
- gateway schema types that need parity with persisted models
acceptance_criteria:
- GoalSpec / TaskSpec / TaskRun types exist with stable JSON contracts.
- Parent/child relationships, owner/assignee fields, expected outputs, acceptance criteria, and budgets are modeled explicitly.
- Unit tests cover JSON round-trips and required-field validation.
notes: Added canonical persistence models for autonomous orchestration in internal/store/state/models.go: GoalSpec, TaskSpec, TaskRun, supporting status/priority enums, authority/budget/result/output helper structs, and Normalize/Validate helpers. Added coverage in internal/store/state/models_test.go for enum parsing, default normalization, validation failures, and JSON round-trip shape stability. Validation: go test ./internal/store/state
close_reason: Implemented

title: Task model: activate kind 38383 task envelopes and shared gateway/control schemas
description:
Turn the reserved AI-Hub-compatible task kind into a real transport envelope.

Scope:
- define the kind 38383 task payload structure and tags
- align control RPC / gateway task request schemas with the same model
- decide which fields live in tags versus content
- document compatibility expectations for future external agents

Likely touchpoints:
- internal/nostr/events/kinds.go
- internal/nostr/runtime/ control/task transport paths
- internal/gateway/methods/schema.go
- cmd/metiqd task/control handlers
acceptance_criteria:
- A concrete 38383 task envelope exists and is used by runtime code, not just constants/docs.
- Gateway/control schemas can serialize and validate the same core task contract.
- Tests cover parse/serialize/sign/verify flows for the task envelope.
notes: Implemented a concrete kind:38383 task envelope for ACP and gateway control flows. Added internal/acp/task_event.go with TaskEnvelope / TaskEventEnvelope plus tag builders, parse/round-trip helpers, and JSON-safe event conversion. Extended ACP TaskPayload, NewTask, DecodeTaskPayload, and pipeline step transport to carry canonical TaskSpec data and embedded task events while preserving legacy instruction fields. Updated gateway ACP dispatch/pipeline schemas to accept and normalize nested task specs, and wired cmd/metiqd ACP dispatch/pipeline handlers to preserve caller-provided task IDs and task contracts. Added tests in internal/acp/task_event_test.go and internal/gateway/methods/schema_test.go. Validation: go test ./internal/acp ./internal/gateway/methods && go test ./cmd/metiqd -run '^$'
close_reason: Implemented

title: Task model: implement task lifecycle state machine and durable state transitions
description:
Add the lifecycle engine that moves tasks and runs through accepted, planned, started, blocked, verifying, completed, failed, and cancelled states.

Scope:
- define valid task/run transitions
- persist transition timestamps, reasons, and actor/source metadata
- reject illegal transitions
- support retries and run attempts under a stable task ID

Likely touchpoints:
- task persistence layer
- cmd/metiqd task execution entrypoints
- lifecycle event emission
acceptance_criteria:
- Illegal lifecycle transitions are rejected consistently.
- A task can have multiple run attempts with durable history.
- Tests cover the allowed transition table and retry semantics.
notes: Implemented the task lifecycle engine in internal/store/state. Added TaskTransition / TaskRunTransition records plus allowed transition tables and ApplyTransition helpers in internal/store/state/task_lifecycle.go, extended TaskSpec / TaskRun with durable transition history and current/last run linkage in models.go, and added DocsRepository persistence helpers for task and task-run documents in docs_repo.go. Tests now cover illegal transitions, retry attempt numbering, and durable task/run round-trips in models_test.go and docs_repo_test.go. Validation: go test ./internal/store/state
close_reason: Implemented

title: Implement fully independent autonomous agent architecture in Swarmstr
description:
Convert the autonomy audit into an implementation program that moves Swarmstr from a strong autonomous agent harness to a fully independent agent architecture.

This epic tracks the work needed to add:
- first-class goal/task/run models
- planning and decomposition
- verification and reviewer loops
- authority, autonomy modes, and governance
- budget enforcement
- workflow recovery and idempotency
- multi-agent task semantics over ACP/Nostr
- task-centric observability and auditability
- episodic/shared memory improvements for autonomous work
- learning and policy evolution

The intent is to preserve Swarmstr's current runtime strengths (tool execution, memory, approvals, observability, Nostr-native routing) while adding the missing control-plane and task-plane contracts required for full independence.
acceptance_criteria:
- Child epics exist for every major workstream identified in the autonomy audit.
- The dependency graph makes the blocking path explicit, especially task schema -> planner/verifier/governance/budget/recovery.
- The final docs/rollout work is blocked on the implementation epics so the hierarchy is execution-ready.
close_reason: All 11 child epics complete. Full autonomous agent architecture implemented across ~60 beads: goal/task/run models (qrx.1), planning engine (qrx.2), verification layer (qrx.3), governance/authority (qrx.4), budget enforcement (qrx.5), workflow reliability (qrx.6), multi-agent collaboration (qrx.7), observability (qrx.8), autonomous memory (qrx.9), learning loop (qrx.10), documentation & rollout (qrx.11). ~530 tests, ~1,889 lines of docs, zero test failures.

title: Apply memory scope to transcript-extracted memories before persistence
description:
Ensure memories created by transcript extraction are scope-aware before they are persisted/indexed.

Why:
- `ExtractFromTurn(...)` output is persisted directly from `cmd/metiqd/main.go`.
- The parity audit did not find an explicit `ApplyScope(...)` step in that path.
- If scope is not attached before persistence, project/local/user filtering can become inconsistent for auto-extracted memories.

Scope:
- Make scope attachment explicit for user and assistant transcript-derived memories
- Verify indexed recall respects scope for extracted memories
- Add regression coverage for user/project/local scope behavior
notes: Made transcript-derived memory scoping explicit by introducing scopedMemoryDocs(...) in cmd/metiqd/memory_scope.go and using it for both user and assistant ExtractFromTurn persistence paths in cmd/metiqd/main.go. This ensures auto-extracted memories carry project/local/user scope keywords before repo/index persistence. Added targeted tests in cmd/metiqd/memory_context_test.go. Validation: go test ./cmd/metiqd -run 'TestScopedMemoryDocs|TestAssembleMemoryRecallContext_FiltersByScope' && go test ./cmd/metiqd -run 'TestAssembleMemorySystemPrompt_IncludesScopedGuidance'
close_reason: Implemented

title: Expose Qdrant degraded state and add repair/backoff fallback semantics
description:
Harden the semantic memory backend so Qdrant/Ollama failures are observable and recoverable instead of silently degrading.

Why:
- `internal/memory/qdrant.go` currently logs search/embed failures and returns nil.
- Hybrid fallback preserves availability but hides persistent semantic-backend failure.
- OpenClaw's memory manager surfaces provider status and fallback/repair state more explicitly.

Scope:
- Track degraded/healthy state for Qdrant-backed search
- Surface when reads are running on fallback only
- Add repair/reopen/backoff behavior for collection/embed/search failures
- Add tests for degraded fallback behavior and recovery
notes: Implemented Qdrant degraded-state observability and recovery semantics. Added internal/memory BackendStatus and StoreStatus, QdrantBackend status reporting, failure-domain-aware cooldown/backoff, collection repair/retry, safer request construction, standalone write failure signaling, and delete preflight semantics. HybridIndex now reports fallback-active state, and cmd/metiqd doctor.memory_status now exposes store health plus count provenance and unsupported-count signaling for qdrant-backed stores. Added focused tests in internal/memory/qdrant_test.go and cmd/metiqd/main_test.go. Validation: go test ./internal/memory ./cmd/metiqd -run 'Test(Qdrant|HybridIndexMemoryStatusReflectsBackendFallback|HandleControlRPCRequest_DoctorMemoryStatusIncludesStoreHealth)'
close_reason: Implemented

title: Integrate session memory into active recall and operator status surfaces
description:
Promote maintained session-memory documents from an out-of-band artifact into the active memory/diagnostic plane.

Why:
- Session memory is generated and updated, but active recall currently emphasizes indexed + file memory.
- Doctor/status output does not clearly expose session-memory health or availability.
- The parity target is tighter runtime integration of continuity artifacts.

Scope:
- Either inject current session-memory summaries into recall/prompt assembly or index them into the main memory search path
- Surface session-memory state in operator diagnostics
- Add tests proving session memory becomes available to future turns
notes: Integrated maintained session-memory documents into active recall and operator diagnostics. buildDynamicMemoryRecallContext/buildAgentRunTurn now add a validated, bounded Session Memory Recall block using the expected scoped session-memory path and logical path identifiers only; unchanged session-memory snapshots are suppressed across future turns via recent recall samples. Extended state.MemoryRecallSample with session-memory metadata/injection telemetry. ensureSessionMemoryCurrent now treats missing, mismatched, or invalid artifacts as stale and preserves transcript checkpoints when refreshes consume no new entries; transcript excerpt assembly now continues until the excerpt budget is filled even when more transcript pages remain. doctor.memory_status now exposes session_memory health/config/runtime counts and only counts usable artifacts. Added focused regressions in cmd/metiqd/memory_context_test.go, cmd/metiqd/session_memory_runtime_test.go, cmd/metiqd/session_lifecycle_test.go, cmd/metiqd/main_test.go, and internal/store/state session-store coverage remains green. Validation: go test ./cmd/metiqd ./internal/store/state -run 'Test(BuildAgentRunTurn_IncludesMaintainedSessionMemoryRecall|BuildDynamicMemoryRecallContext|HandleControlRPCRequest_DoctorMemoryStatusIncludes(SessionMemoryHealth|StoreHealth)|MemoryRecallSample_JSONShape|SessionStore_RecordMemoryRecall_MergesAndCaps|SessionMemoryNeedsRefresh_WhenArtifactPathMismatches|SessionMemoryExtractOnce_PreservesCheckpointWhenNoNewTranscriptEntries|SessionMemoryBuildTranscriptExcerpt_ContinuesBeyondFirstLineWhenPageHasMore)'
close_reason: Implemented

title: Harden plugin loading with provenance, path safety, and schema validation
description:
Strengthen plugin manager trust boundaries before treating Swarmstr's plugin runtime as parity-complete.

Why:
- `internal/plugins/manager/manager.go` trusts configured `install_path` and resolves entry scripts with limited validation.
- The parity audit found weaker provenance/trust controls than OpenClaw's plugin loader.

Scope:
- Validate install paths and reject unsafe traversal/out-of-root cases as appropriate
- Add manifest/config schema validation before registration
- Improve diagnostics for load/validation failures
- Add tests for invalid plugin manifests, bad paths, and unsupported entry resolution
notes: Hardened plugin loading in internal/plugins/manager/manager.go by requiring trusted install paths (managed extensions or configured plugins.load_paths), checking install-path provenance against plugins.installs records, constraining entry-point resolution to stay within the plugin root, and returning aggregated load diagnostics instead of silently accepting unsafe configs. Tightened manifest validation in internal/plugins/sdk/api.go and applied it in both Goja and Node runtimes, with Node defaulting missing manifest IDs from the entry path for compatibility. Added regression coverage in internal/plugins/manager/manager_test.go and internal/plugins/runtime/goja_host_test.go. Validation: go test ./internal/plugins/manager ./internal/plugins/runtime && go test ./cmd/metiqd -run '^$'
close_reason: Implemented

title: Implement OpenClaw parity audit follow-ups for Swarmstr harness maturity
description:
Create and track the implementation follow-ups identified by the OpenClaw vs Swarmstr parity audit.

Themes covered by children:
- tool-loop correctness
- memory scope / retrieval / status parity
- plugin trust and validation
- prompt-injection hardening for untrusted external content
- final documentation and parity audit updates

Use this epic as the parent for the concrete implementation beads below.
close_reason: all steps complete

title: Fix tool-loop detector poll-gating parity bug
description:
Port OpenClaw's known-poll classification into `internal/agent/toolloop/detection.go` so `KnownPollNoProgress` only applies to real polling-style tools.

Why:
- Current detection comments say generic repeat is for non-poll tools, but the code does not gate known-poll handling the way OpenClaw does.
- This can misclassify non-poll tools as stuck polling loops.

Scope:
- Add the equivalent of `isKnownPollToolCall(...)`
- Use it for both warning and critical known-poll branches
- Keep generic-repeat behavior for non-poll tools
- Add regression tests covering poll and non-poll calls
notes: Implemented OpenClaw-style poll gating in internal/agent/toolloop/detection.go; restricted KnownPollNoProgress to command_status and process actions poll/log. Updated detection and agent loop tests for known-poll and non-poll paths. Validation: go test ./internal/agent/toolloop && go test ./internal/agent/...
close_reason: Implemented

title: Add regression coverage for untested OpenClaw-derived channel plugins
description: Swarmstr currently ships multiple channel implementations derived from or modeled on OpenClaw, but only 7 of 18 extensions have tests under internal/extensions. The largest parity gap is in the untested overlap transports: discord, slack, telegram, whatsapp, line, and bluebubbles. OpenClaw has substantial transport-specific coverage for these paths (threading, webhook handling, reply routing, payload normalization, etc.), while Swarmstr has little or none. Scope: add focused regression tests around connect/config validation, inbound normalization, outbound send/edit/thread behavior, webhook signature/verification handling where applicable, and the concrete bugs tracked in the related channel beads.
notes:
First tranche landed: added regression coverage for slack, discord, and telegram inbound thread/reply metadata while fixing swarmstr-t1v. Remaining coverage work is still open for the other untested OpenClaw-derived channels.
Added Telegram webhook-mode regression coverage while fixing swarmstr-olj (setWebhook/deleteWebhook path, registry dispatch, secret-token verification).
Added WhatsApp regression coverage while fixing swarmstr-sza (shared webhook routing, signed webhook verification, phone_number_id filtering, reply-target send routing).
close_reason: Added regression coverage for bluebubbles (20 tests), nextcloud (23 tests), and synology (21 tests) — covering config validation, poll/webhook message delivery, sender filtering, dedup, send API, close idempotency, and error handling

title: Telegram channel plugin documents webhook_url but always uses polling
description: The Telegram extension schema and comments advertise config.webhook_url as an optional transport that should switch the plugin from getUpdates polling to webhook delivery. Current Connect() ignores webhook_url entirely and always starts bot.poll(ctx). This is a concrete config/behavior mismatch and a parity gap versus OpenClaw's dedicated telegram webhook path and tests. Scope: either implement webhook mode (registration, verification, shutdown, tests) or remove the dead config surface until it exists.
close_reason: Implemented Telegram webhook mode, admin webhook routing, and regression tests.

title: WhatsApp channel plugin is not safe for multi-instance routing
description: The WhatsApp extension starts an embedded HTTP server per channel instance, defaults every instance to the same listen address (:8080), and binds a fixed /webhook path. Multiple WhatsApp channels in one process will conflict on listen/bind. Separately, Send() falls back to a mutable default_recipient that is seeded from the first inbound sender, which makes replies ambiguous in multi-user scenarios. OpenClaw handles channel/account routing with shared registry infrastructure instead of per-instance listeners. Scope: move WhatsApp webhook handling behind shared routing/registration, make instance identification explicit, and remove implicit recipient routing from Send() or make it session-targeted and tested.
close_reason: Moved WhatsApp to shared webhook routing, removed implicit recipient fallback, and added signed webhook tests.

title: Channel thread metadata parity is missing for Discord/Slack/Telegram
description: Swarmstr marks discord, slack, and telegram as Threads-capable, and each implementation exposes SendInThread, but inbound messages do not populate sdk.InboundChannelMessage.ThreadID or ReplyToEventID. In practice that means the runtime cannot recover thread/reply context from inbound events, even though OpenClaw has explicit thread-context handling and tests for these transports (for example slack/threading tests, discord monitor thread handling, telegram thread/webhook tests). Scope: capture the upstream thread identifiers from each provider (Slack thread_ts, Discord thread channel / reply reference, Telegram reply_to_message_id or topic/thread metadata where available), wire them into InboundChannelMessage, and add regression tests for inbound threaded messages.
close_reason: Implemented inbound thread/reply metadata for Slack, Discord, and Telegram and added regression tests.

title: Provider-aware transcript policy parity for prompt hardening
description: Follow-up to swarmstr-ekn. Swarmstr now has shared prompt-literal sanitization, bootstrap budgeting, unified prompt assembly, stronger history sanitization, and tool-result guards. Remaining parity gap vs OpenClaw is provider-aware transcript policy / session transcript repair (e.g. provider-specific ordering constraints, richer tool-call ID/name normalization policies, and provider-specific history repair rules).
close_reason: Implemented provider-aware transcript policy parity: 8-step pipeline with role alternation enforcement (Anthropic/Gemini), mid-system message stripping, consecutive role merging, leading-user requirement, empty content fill, and per-provider tool-call ID length constraints. 49 tests covering all new steps + full integration. Updated Gemini chat test for new ordering.

title: Add LLM heartbeat turn runner with per-agent heartbeat.model override
description: swarmstr heartbeat is currently NIP-38 presence/status only, so there is no LLM heartbeat response path to apply a model override to. Implement an OpenClaw-style heartbeat turn runner (or the swarmstr equivalent), thread agents[].heartbeat.model through the execution path, define delivery/bootstrap semantics, and add regression coverage.
close_reason: Done

title: Add dynamic light-model routing and compaction model selection
description: Implemented heuristic light-model routing fixes in swarmstr, including provider-aware light model construction, tool-history/image-aware routing, channels.join runtime selection, explicit light-model use for sessions.compact summaries, config validation, docs, and tests. This issue tracks the completed implementation tranche landed in this session.
close_reason: Completed

title: Reconcile MCP tool registry during live config changes
description: Discovered while implementing swarmstr-3p3.2. MCP runtime state now updates live via manager.ApplyConfig on config mutation/file reload, but the agent tool registry is still primarily startup-registered. New/changed MCP tools may not become provider-visible immediately, and removed/disabled MCP tools can remain registered until restart. Follow-up should either (a) add safe MCP tool registry reconciliation on live config changes, including removal, or (b) make MCP tool-surface changes explicitly restart-gated and surface that consistently. This was intentionally deferred to keep swarmstr-3p3.2 focused on connection state/lifecycle.
close_reason: Implemented live MCP tool registry reconciliation and per-turn tool snapshots

title: MCPAUTH-01 Add OAuth and credential lifecycle for remote MCP servers
description:
Recommendation anchor: MCPAUTH-01 in docs/refactor/src-mcp-transfer-recommendations.md.

Add first-class OAuth and credential lifecycle support for remote SSE/HTTP MCP servers.

Why this bead exists:
- `src` does not treat remote MCP auth as a manual header problem. It has OAuth discovery, token storage, refresh/revoke, step-up handling, and client-secret persistence (`src/services/mcp/auth.ts:325-620`, `src/services/mcp/auth.ts:847-1354`, `src/services/mcp/auth.ts:2362-2445`).
- `src/tools/McpAuthTool/McpAuthTool.ts:28-215` also provides a model-visible auth bootstrap path for servers that exist but are not yet usable.
- swarmstr currently only supports static request headers in `internal/mcp/manager.go`, which blocks many modern remote MCP servers from working reliably.

Scope:
- Add credential persistence and retrieval for remote MCP servers.
- Support OAuth/browser callback lifecycle for supported transports.
- Surface a `needs-auth` state and a user-triggerable auth entrypoint (tool or control action) that can swap real tools in after successful auth.

Non-goals:
- Do not copy `src`'s UI components.
- Do not require Claude.ai-specific connector behavior.
design:
Depends on MCPCFG-01 and MCPCONN-01.

Source anchors:
- src/services/mcp/auth.ts:325-620,847-1354,2362-2445
- src/tools/McpAuthTool/McpAuthTool.ts:28-215

Target touchpoints:
- swarmstr/internal/mcp/manager.go
- swarmstr/internal/secrets/secrets.go
- swarmstr/cmd/metiqd/main.go
- control/CLI surfaces for auth initiation
acceptance_criteria:
- Remote SSE/HTTP MCP servers can store and reuse credentials without embedding long-lived secrets directly in plain config.
- swarmstr can represent and recover from `needs-auth` state for supported servers.
- Auth completion can trigger reconnect and tool/capability refresh automatically.
- Refresh/revoke/clear paths are covered by tests or equivalent integration coverage.
close_reason: Implemented MCP OAuth and credential lifecycle for remote servers

title: MCPCAP-01 Add generic external MCP resources support
description:
Recommendation anchor: MCPCAP-01 in docs/refactor/src-mcp-transfer-recommendations.md.

Add generic external MCP `resources/list` and `resources/read` support to swarmstr's runtime.

Why this bead exists:
- `src` treats generic MCP resources as a first-class part of the product and exposes them through shared MCP resource tools (`src/services/mcp/client.ts:1998-2027`, `src/tools/ListMcpResourcesTool/ListMcpResourcesTool.ts:1-123`, `src/tools/ReadMcpResourceTool/ReadMcpResourceTool.ts:1-158`).
- swarmstr already has ContextVM-specific resource tools, but external MCP servers connected through `internal/mcp/manager.go` still only contribute callable tools.
- This leaves a large part of the MCP protocol unusable for external servers and blocks parity with MCP servers that publish documents/resources rather than only tools.

Scope:
- Teach the external MCP manager to list and read generic MCP resources.
- Expose generic external resource access through Go-native built-in tools or equivalent runtime surfaces.
- Keep external MCP resources distinct from ContextVM resources at the protocol/runtime layer.

Non-goals:
- Do not collapse ContextVM and external MCP into one transport abstraction.
- Do not add prompt support here.
design:
Depends on MCPCONN-01.

Source anchors:
- src/services/mcp/client.ts:1998-2027
- src/tools/ListMcpResourcesTool/ListMcpResourcesTool.ts:1-123
- src/tools/ReadMcpResourceTool/ReadMcpResourceTool.ts:1-158

Target touchpoints:
- swarmstr/internal/mcp/manager.go
- swarmstr/internal/agent/toolbuiltin/
- swarmstr/internal/agent/tools.go
acceptance_criteria:
- External MCP servers that advertise resources can be queried for `resources/list` and `resources/read` through swarmstr.
- The runtime exposes generic external MCP resource access via stable built-in tooling, not just raw internal methods.
- Resource results preserve server identity and enough metadata for downstream use.
- Tests cover capability-gated behavior and resource-read error handling.
close_reason: Implemented generic external MCP resources support

title: MCPUX-01 Add operator-facing MCP management methods and CLI
description:
Recommendation anchor: MCPUX-01 in docs/refactor/src-mcp-transfer-recommendations.md.

Add real MCP management methods and CLI affordances so operators do not have to hand-edit raw config and restart the daemon for every MCP change.

Why this bead exists:
- `src/cli/handlers/mcp.tsx:26-360` provides a genuine operational surface for add/list/get/remove/test/import/reset operations with health inspection.
- swarmstr currently exposes external MCP through startup config parsing and tool registration only.
- Without operator-facing MCP management, the external MCP feature is difficult to use, inspect, and debug in practice.

Scope:
- Add gateway/control methods and CLI entrypoints for MCP list/get/add/remove/test/reconnect style operations.
- Surface health/capability/auth state in command responses.
- Prefer swarmstr-native gateway methods and `metiq` CLI surfaces over copying `src`'s exact subcommand tree.

Non-goals:
- Do not copy React/Ink UI flows.
- Desktop-config import can be deferred unless it fits naturally after the core CRUD/test path exists.
design:
Depends on MCPCFG-01 and MCPCONN-01.

Source anchors:
- src/cli/handlers/mcp.tsx:26-360

Target touchpoints:
- swarmstr/cmd/metiq/cli_cmds.go
- swarmstr/internal/gateway/methods/
- swarmstr/internal/gateway/protocol/
- swarmstr/internal/mcp/manager.go
acceptance_criteria:
- Operators can inspect configured MCP servers and their current health/state without reading raw config or logs.
- Operators can add/remove/test/reconnect MCP servers through supported methods/CLI entrypoints.
- Responses include enough server metadata to debug auth/state/capability issues.
- CLI and gateway method coverage includes tests or equivalent integration checks.
close_reason: Implemented operator-facing MCP management methods and CLI

title: Port src MCP server lifecycle and capability features into swarmstr
description:
Parent tracking epic for the MCP-specific transfer plan documented in docs/refactor/src-mcp-transfer-recommendations.md.

The audit found that swarmstr already has basic external MCP tool invocation through `internal/mcp/manager.go` and `cmd/metiqd/main.go`, but it is still missing most of the higher-value MCP product/runtime surface that exists in `src`: layered config resolution, stateful connection lifecycle, OAuth and credential handling, generic resources/prompts support, operator-facing MCP management, and structured lifecycle telemetry.

This epic exists to close that gap without importing `src`'s UI-heavy implementation details. The goal is Go-native parity for the MCP features that materially improve swarmstr's external MCP usability and reliability.
design:
